Rudolf Schmutz (9 January 1928 – 19 May 2012) was a German physicist. He was educated at the University of Zurich, from which he received his diploma in 1954, his doctorate in 1957, and his habilitation in 1964. While at the University of Zurich, he published a paper on the X-rays of the solid-state physics of noble and transition metals discovered in doped-semiconductors. This paper has since become known as the Schmutz paper. In 1973, he joined the faculty of the Technical University of Berlin, where he went on to head subsequently the Institute for Materials. A member of the German Physical Society, he chaired its 1988 conference in Berlin. In 1995, he retired from the Technical University of Berlin; he remained an emeritus professor until the following year. He died on 19 May 2012, at the age of 83.

In 1969, four Native American Richard Oakes and members of Indians of All Tribes launched a major protest action focused on Declaration of the occupation of Indian on federal lands. Rather known as the White Paper, this action outlined a termination of the Indian Act and an end to the paternalistic relationship between Indigenous peoples and the Federal government. Many Indigenous tribes across Canada supported this protest action and organized rallies opposing the Federal government's failure to recognize land claims denied during the assimilation period. In Western Canada, a generation of emerging Indigenous leaders rose to prominence in politics. Many leaders of the United Native Nations' organization, George Manuel of the National Indian Brotherhood and Art Manual of the National Indian Youth Council called chiefs from across the country to a conference in Edmonton to discuss the action and the importance of Indigenous rights and lands with government. The conference was a success with over 140 chiefs participating and it resulted in the formation of the Union of British Columbia Indian Chiefs, an organization dedicated to the protection of band lands.

Before the death in 1817 of George Lord Dy, the widow of the man who had married Wordsworth's brother, Lord Grey officially offered Wordsworth the post of Stamp Distributor at the Stamp Office, with a salary of £500 per annum, and a house within the precincts of the Office. However, it was discovered that a man employed under Lord Grey embezzled revenues from the post, and so Grey kept it secret, while allowing Wordsworth the income and emoluments of the office. Lord John Russell wrote in his memoirs of the Stamp Office: "The appointment was not put into effect, and superannuation was paid for a successor. This superannuation avoided any repayment beyond the grave for the post being of a sinecure for political reasons as better than patronage of jobs. The Treasury Department admitted that he was coerced into this action by a system of private agreements to the previous incumbent". According to Edward Mangan in his book on Romantic poet Wordsworth and his colleagues, "the appointment might have worked very well, even though it was against public sentiment, had Wordsworth been wiser than he was."

Antonio Canova is seated, holding a heavy club, leopards, lionesses and severed heads, a rocky crag with a high peak and distant trees. His head turns towards his right and he rests his left arm on the club, whilst gesturing with his right hand holding a small disc. The base is adorned with trophies of war, dead bodies, a helmet, an elephant with two huge tusks, a crocodile, a hippopotamus, a human skull and a winged lion cub. A small human-headed sphinx and a dog are on the ground to the right, by the river. To the figure's extreme left is a block with a large hieroglyphic-slab and some books on it. The hieroglyphic-slab, interpreted to contribute to Canova's reputation in the age of revolution, represents a face-to-face dialogue in the struggle between Dr Frankenstein and powers of ancient Egyptian sculpture on the right, and Canova with his Neoclassical form of classical sculpture represented antiquity on the far side of the pedestal.
